hook: A 1929 dock pumphouse beside a reborn timber pond
facts: Built 1929 by the Port of London Authority to keep Surrey Docks topped up with water
Lavender Pond once floated imported timber to stop it cracking
Nature park created 1981; the building held the Rotherhithe Heritage Museum until 2011
One of the last structures left from the Surrey Commercial Docks
w: They built this brick pumphouse in 1929 to keep the vast Surrey timber docks brimming with water. The pond beside it once floated imported logs so they wouldn't crack in the air. Docks gone, pumps stilled, the lot reborn in 1981 as reedbed and marsh. Industry, rewilded.

hook: A 1912 tiled tube under the Thames for Arsenal workers
facts: Opened 26 October 1912, 504 metres long, roughly ten feet below the riverbed
Built so south-bank workers could reach the docks and Royal Arsenal in all weather
Both Edwardian rotunda entrances are Grade II listed
The Arsenal employed around 80,000 people at its WWI peak
w: Down a domed Edwardian rotunda, then a white-tiled tube boring 504 metres under the Thames, ten feet below the riverbed. Opened 1912 so munitions workers could reach the Royal Arsenal in any weather, dry-shod. Eighty thousand once toiled across the water. Walk it; the river hums overhead.

hook: A wild bronze tsar where he wrecked Evelyn's house
facts: Erected 2000, unveiled 2001, marking 300 years since Peter's Grand Embassy
Sculpted by Mihail Chemiakin with a throne, a dwarf and a cannon
Peter lodged at nearby Sayes Court in 1698 to study shipbuilding
He and his men trashed John Evelyn's house and famous garden
w: A lurching bronze tsar, a throne, a cannon and a tiny dwarf, all by Chemiakin. In 1698 Peter the Great lodged nearby at Sayes Court to learn shipbuilding, then he and his crew wrecked John Evelyn's house and wheelbarrowed through his prize hedges. London's strangest statue marks the rampage.

hook: An architect's mock-Bastille on the hill above Greenwich
facts: Designed and built by playwright-architect John Vanbrugh around 1719 for himself
Said to echo the Paris Bastille, where Vanbrugh spent years jailed as a suspected spy
Gothic keep and towers, a deliberate break from his baroque Blenheim and Castle Howard
Later an RAF orphans' school; now private flats
legend: Locals long called it Bastille House after Vanbrugh's French cell
w: The man who built Blenheim raised himself a toy fortress here around 1719, all turrets and arrow-slit windows. Locals dubbed it Bastille House, the legend being Vanbrugh modelled it on the Paris cell where he'd been locked up for years as a suspected spy. Baroque genius, gone gleefully gothic.

hook: Bill Sikes met his end in Dickens's foulest slum
facts: Tidal ditches off the Thames once ringed this Bermondsey block, near Mill Street
Dickens set Bill Sikes's death here in Oliver Twist, above 'Folly Ditch'
Henry Mayhew called it a 'pest island' smelling of the graveyard
Slum cleared in the 1860s; warehouses and flats stand now
w: Tidal ditches once ringed this block of rotting tenements, the water thick with carcasses and filth. Mayhew called it a pest island that smelled of the grave. Dickens sent Bill Sikes to his death here, swinging from a rooftop above Folly Ditch. Cleared in the 1860s; the dread name lingers.

hook: Hawksmoor's church on the spot a saint was clubbed to death
facts: Rebuilt by Nicholas Hawksmoor 1712-1714 after the medieval roof fell in
Marks where Archbishop Alfege was murdered by Danish raiders in 1012
Killed with ox bones after he refused to let a ransom be paid
Henry VIII baptised here; Thomas Tallis buried within
legend: Tradition holds the drunken Danes pelted him with ox bones before an axe finished him
w: Hawksmoor's great Portland-stone box, rebuilt 1714 after the old roof caved. It stands where Danish raiders murdered Archbishop Alfege in 1012, legend telling that a drunken mob pelted him with ox bones before an axe ended it. Henry VIII was christened here; Tallis lies beneath. Blood under the floor.

hook: A giant anchor locals fought the council to keep
facts: Placed on Deptford High Street in 1988, brought from Chatham Dockyard
Honours Henry VIII's Deptford Royal Dockyard, England's first
Council removed it in 2013, blaming street drinking
Returned in 2018 after a 4,000-signature 'Give Us Back Our Bloomin' Anchor' campaign
w: A vast iron anchor planted on the high street in 1988, a nod to Henry VIII's Royal Dockyard that launched the navy from here. The council hauled it off in 2013, blaming drinkers. Deptford revolted: four thousand signed 'Give Us Back Our Bloomin' Anchor', and back it came in 2018. Local pride, cast in iron.

hook: Twin Victorian burial grounds split by an invisible line
facts: Both opened in 1858, one Anglican, one for nonconformists, divided by a hedge
Early examples of London's first wave of public cemeteries
Hold over 600 war graves between the two halves
Cat-painter Horatio Couldery and many a 'public grave' lie here
w: Two cemeteries opened in 1858, side by side but divided by faith: Anglican Brockley, nonconformist Ladywell, an invisible line down the middle. Pauper 'public graves' went unmarked, shared with strangers. Now a wild green sweep of leaning stones, foxes and over six hundred war dead. Quietly enormous.

hook: A back-street ale house with a rare bar billiards table
facts: Recorded by name in Old Bailey proceedings as far back as 1814
Fifteen handpumps plus a deep range of Belgian bottles
Bar billiards table out the back, an increasingly rare survivor
Holds an annual Pickle Festival and long-running CAMRA honours
w: Tucked down a Deptford back lane, a name in the Old Bailey records by 1814. Fifteen handpumps, a wall of Belgian bottles, and out the back a bar billiards table, almost extinct elsewhere. Once a year the regulars throw a Pickle Festival. Defiantly itself while everything around it changes.

hook: Where Waterloo's victory was flashed toward London
facts: A semaphore station crowned the hill around 1795 on the Admiralty's London-Deal line
News of Wellington's win at Waterloo relayed past this summit
Victorian park opened 1895 on Haberdashers' Company land
A grassed mound near the top hides an 1874 covered reservoir
w: Around 1795 a semaphore tower stood on this summit, one shutter-station in the Admiralty chain flashing signals between London and the coast. Word of Waterloo passed this way. The tower's long gone, the name stayed, and the steep upper park still hands you the City skyline over the rooftops. Old signals, fresh view.

hook: Four chimneys cut short to spare the Observatory's sky
facts: Built from 1902 to power London's electric trams
Its first chimneys were trimmed after Royal Observatory objections
Coal arrived by barge to a jetty on sixteen Doric iron columns
Now the Underground's standby emergency power source
w: Four brick chimneys loom over riverside Greenwich, built from 1902 to run London's trams. The astronomers up the hill complained the smoke and height fouled their sightlines, so the stacks were lopped shorter. Coal once barged to a jetty on sixteen Doric iron columns. Still wired as the Tube's last-ditch backup.

hook: A working millennium sundial of Highland granite
facts: Twelve granite boulders raised on the spring equinox, 21 March 2000
Stones hauled from Mount Struie in the Scottish Highlands
Designed by sculptor Richard Kindersley to work as a giant sundial
A community project of the Brockley Society, Civic Trust commended
w: Twelve granite boulders dragged down from a Highland hillside and set in a ring on the spring equinox of 2000, the stones aligned so the circle tells the time by shadow. Not ancient at all, a millennium folly by sculptor Kindersley, dreamed up by locals. Hilltop neo-megalith with a fine view thrown in.

hook: London's oldest wet dock, named for the whalers
facts: Laid out 1695-1699, the city's oldest surviving riverside wet dock
Greenland whalers boiled blubber to oil in quayside houses here
Whalebone went into corsets and umbrella ribs
Surrounding streets still read Finland, Norway, Onega, Brunswick
w: Dug at the close of the 1690s, the oldest wet dock left on the Thames. Whaling ships limped back from the Arctic to boil blubber into lamp oil in quayside houses, the bones sold off for corsets and umbrella ribs. Now still water and yachts, but the street names, Norway, Finland, Onega, keep the cold trade alive.

hook: A pocket park holding three thousand reburied sailors
facts: Chosen in 1857 as a new burial ground for Greenwich Pensioners
Around 3,000 sailors' remains moved here in 1875 for railway and hall building
Includes men who fought at Trafalgar and in the Crimea
Made a public park in 1926
w: A leafy local park with a secret weight: when the navy needed land in 1875, some three thousand Greenwich Pensioners were dug up and carted here, men who'd stood at Trafalgar and in the Crimea. Seamen to the west plot, officers east, even in death by rank. Children play above an old man-of-war's crew.
